reason: The item cleanly tests boundary-aware reasoning about what TNT-equivalent energy can and cannot justify, and the non-implications around activation energy versus total explosive energy release. Decoys are largely single-fault and engineering-plausible (mechanism overclaim, single-metric interchangeability, and distance-scaling overreach), yielding good expert discrimination. Minor ambiguity remains because “hazard tier” and “dominant energy source differs” are somewhat underspecified, which can introduce interpretive latitude even if the intended boundary is clear.

The item cleanly targets a single expert judgment: why cooling-sensitivity (SADT improvement) cannot be used to infer baseline uncooled safety, and why initiation/kinetics (activation energy) is the more relevant hazard dimension than TNT-equivalent. However, because it is short-answer without structured options, the diagnostic separation depends heavily on rubric strictness (e.g., how much mention of SOC-conditional ignition threshold is required), which weakens decoy/contrast effects and reduces discriminative consistency across graders.
